name: Vachaspati Misra
dates_notes: Polymath; dates fixed by his own colophons; commented across six darśanas. The Nyāya-Sūcī-Nibandha colophon gives a date readable as either 841 CE (Vikrama era) or 976 CE (Śaka era); modern scholarship (Stcherbatsky; Potter) splits, with the corpus adopting the later reading consistent with Mīmāṃsā citations.

lineage_polemical.note: Vāchaspati and Haribhadra Sūri are near-contemporary cross-doxographic engagers — Vāchaspati's commentary-corpus across six darśanas represents the Brahmanical equivalent of Haribhadra's Ṣaḍ-Darśana-Samuccaya, and the relation is mutual cross-traditional engagement on the methodology of fair-minded systematic exposition of rival schools.

core_thesis: Where does *avidyā* (positive ignorance) reside? Vāchaspati Miśra's answer founds the *Bhāmatī* sub-school: the *jīva* is its locus (*āśraya*); *Brahman* is only its content (*viṣaya*). Ignorances are accordingly plural, one per *jīva*, and the *jīva* functions as the immediate seat of *adhyāsa* (superimposition) even though it is ultimately non-different from Brahman. Two consequences follow that the rival Vivaraṇa line will spend centuries contesting. First, Īśvara's cosmic *māyā* and the *jīva*'s individual avidyā are analytically distinct, since their loci differ. Second, *karma* enters the path to liberation in a qualified way: it cannot produce *jñāna*, but the prepared *antaḥkaraṇa* without which *śravaṇa* fails to mature into *aparokṣa-jñāna* is itself a karmic achievement, and *manana* and *nididhyāsana* are read as *prasaṅkhyāna*-like cultivations that ripen the rise of immediate cognition. Vāchaspati's commentaries across six *darśanas* — Advaita, Yoga, Nyāya, Mīmāṃsā, Sāṃkhya, Vyākaraṇa — earn him the epithet *Ṣaḍ-Darśanī-Vallabha*; the *Bhāmatī* itself sets the agenda for the post-Śaṅkara dialectic.

engaged_works.title: Bhamati
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored gloss on Śaṅkara's Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya; the Bhāmatī school's founding text.
engaged_works.summary: Sub-commentary (*ṭīkā*) on Śaṅkara's *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya*, named after Vāchaspati's wife and tracking the four *adhyāyas* of the *Sūtras*; the long *upodghāta* on the *adhyāsa-bhāṣya* is the locus classicus for the *Bhāmatī* analysis of superimposition. The doctrinal commitments of an entire sub-school are made here. Avidyā is located in the *jīva*, not in Brahman; the standard *Bhāmatī* warrant is the analysis at BSB 1.4.3 and 4.1.3, where the asymmetry between cosmic *māyā* (Īśvara's *upādhi*) and individual avidyā (the *jīva*'s) is pressed into a doctrinal distinction. *Jñāna-karma-samuccaya* is admitted in the qualified sense that *karma* produces the *cittaśuddhi* without which *śravaṇa* will not yield *aparokṣa-jñāna*; *manana* and *nididhyāsana* are read as *prasaṅkhyāna*-style cultivations whose end is the rise of immediate intuitive knowledge from the Mahāvākya. The text spawns the principal *Bhāmatī*-line apparatus — Amalānanda's *Kalpataru*, Appayya Dīkṣita's *Parimala* — and provides the position against which Prakāśātman's *Vivaraṇa* defines itself.

engaged_works.title: Tattva-Vaisharadi
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed in Vāchaspati's mature period; relative chronology with the Bhāmatī uncertain.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored sub-commentary on Vyāsa's Yoga-Bhāṣya on Patañjali's Yoga-Sūtras.
engaged_works.summary: Sub-commentary on the *Yoga-Sūtra*–*Vyāsa-Bhāṣya* complex; for several centuries the standard pre-modern *ṭīkā* on Patañjali through Vyāsa. Vāchaspati fixes the philosophical reading of the disputed *sūtras*: the analysis of *citta-vṛtti-nirodha*, the structure of *samādhi*, the architecture of the eight *aṅgas*, the *kaivalya*-doctrine of the fourth *pāda*. He defends the twenty-five-*tattva* Sāṃkhya-Yoga ontology of *puruṣa* and *prakṛti*. He also clarifies the precise sense in which the *Yoga* school's theism — *īśvara* as a special *puruṣa* untouched by *kleśa-karma-vipāka-āśaya* — differs from both atheistic Sāṃkhya and the cosmologically creative *Īśvara* of theistic Vedānta. For Vāchaspati's stature the work matters as evidence of range. The same author who founded *Bhāmatī*-Advaita commanded the technical idiom of an opposed *darśana*; the polymathic authority by which his Advaita rulings carry weight is exhibited here in operation. Bhojadeva's *Rāja-Mārtaṇḍa* and Vijñānabhikṣu's *Yoga-Vārttika* both engage the *Vaiśāradī* as standard reference.

engaged_works.title: Nyaya-Varttika-Tatparya-Tika
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored sub-commentary on Uddyotakara's Nyāya-Vārttika; foundational to later Naiyāyika scholasticism.
engaged_works.summary: Sub-commentary on Uddyotakara's *Nyāya-Vārttika*, which is itself a commentary on Vātsyāyana's *Nyāya-Bhāṣya* on the *Nyāya-Sūtras*. The *Tātparya-Ṭīkā* fixes the standard medieval reading of the entire *Nyāya-Sūtra* commentarial stack and is foundational to later Naiyāyika scholasticism. Udayana's *Tātparya-Pariśuddhi* takes it as direct base; the Navya-Nyāya tradition reads the *Sūtras* through Vāchaspati's interpretation. Major doctrinal contributions: the treatment of *pramāṇa-svarūpa* and *prāmāṇya-vāda*; the analysis of *vyāpti* and the *hetv-ābhāsa* classification; the defence of *paramāṇu*-atomism against Diṅnāga and Dharmakīrti; the analysis of *śabda-pramāṇa* in dialogue with Mīmāṃsā. For Vāchaspati's authority within Vedānta the work matters because the Naiyāyika opponent the *Bhāmatī* engages, and the Naiyāyika tradition the *Bhāmatī* must distinguish itself from, both run substantially through Vāchaspati's own *Tātparya-Ṭīkā*.
